title: SpaceX IPO drama
summary: A video discussing SpaceX's potential IPO as one of history's largest public offerings and its broader implications for Tesla, the space economy, satellite internet, and defense contracts. The analysis covers how Starlink affects SpaceX's valuation and why investors are closely monitoring the company.
writeup: The video traces how the legal and governance battles Elon Musk fought over Tesla's $56 billion compensation package in Delaware directly shaped the structure of the upcoming SpaceX AI IPO. A Delaware judge voided the package in 2024 despite two separate shareholder votes approving it at roughly 73%, and the experience drove Musk to design SpaceX AI's share structure so no analogous outcome is possible. The through-line is corporate governance as both a retrospective lesson and a forward-looking shield, with the same coalition of large pension funds now opposing SpaceX AI that previously opposed the Tesla package.
key_points: The SpaceX AI IPO is expected within roughly one month of recording and could reach a $2 trillion valuation, which would dwarf the prior record set by Saudi Aramco's 2019 IPO at $29 billion.

A Delaware Chancery Court judge voided Musk's $56 billion Tesla pay package after a shareholder derivative suit filed by a holder of nine Tesla shares, even though 73% of non-Musk shareholders approved it initially and 72% approved it again on a second vote with fuller disclosures.

SpaceX AI will be incorporated in Texas, not Delaware; Texas's SB 21 reforms and a 3% shareholding threshold for derivative suits provide the legal framework that makes a repeat of the Delaware outcome structurally impossible.

Musk holds 42% of SpaceX equity but controls 79% of voting power through class B shares, with a provision that the CEO can only be removed by a class B shareholder vote, meaning removal requires Musk's own agreement unless he sells a substantial portion of his shares.

The three pension funds leading opposition to the SpaceX AI governance structure, including CalPERS, the New York State Comptroller, and the New York City Comptroller, collectively manage over $1 trillion in assets but were in the minority against the Tesla compensation packages they also opposed.

Tesla's Delaware Supreme Court ultimately reversed the McCormick decision in 2025 and restored the original $56 billion package; Tesla shareholders subsequently approved a new, larger compensation package tied to milestones including an $8.5 trillion market cap, 20 million vehicles sold, 1 million robot taxis deployed, 1 million Optimus robots delivered, and $400 billion in adjusted EBITDA.
